subject: Science
description: Create a product from the reused materials that will meet a human need (e.g., pencil holder, musical instrument, bird feeder). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the product.

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, develop questions to conduct a structured investigation to determine ways to conserve Earth's resources (i.e., reduce, reuse, and recycle) and communicate results.

subject: Science
description: Participate in a teacher-led activity to gather, organize and record recyclable materials data on a chart or table using technology. Communicate results.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how humans use Earth's resources.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science
description: Develop a device (i.e., umbrella, shade structure, or hat) which would reduce heat from the sun (temperature) using an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the device.

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, develop questions to conduct a structured investigation to determine how sunlight affects the temperature of the Earth's natural resources (e.g., sand, soil, rocks, and water).

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, generate and answer questions to develop a simple model, which describes observable patterns of sunlight on the Earth's surface (day and night).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that the Sun provides the Earth with heat and light.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation of the pattern of the Earth's seasonal changes in the environment using evidence from observations.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the pattern of seasonal changes on the Earth.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Universe

subject: Science
description: Explain why things may not work the same if some of the parts are missing.

subject: Science
description: Analyze a large composite structure to describe its smaller components using drawing and writing.

subject: Science
description: Use basic shapes and spatial reasoning to model large objects in the environment using a set of small objects (e.g., blocks, construction sets).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how solid objects can be constructed from a smaller set.

subject: Science
description: Describe and compare the properties of different materials (e.g., wood, plastic, metal, cloth, paper) and classify these materials by their observable characteristics (visual, aural, or natural textural) and by their physical properties (weight, volume, solid or liquid, and sink or float).

subject: Science
description: Generate questions and investigate the differences between liquids and solids and develop awareness that a liquid can become a solid and vice versa.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the solid and liquid states of matter.

subject: Science
description: Organization of Matter and Chemical Interactions

subject: Science
description: Observe and report how some present-day animals resemble extinct animals (i.e., elephants resemble wooly mammoths).

subject: Science
description: Obtain information from informational text or other media to document and report examples of different plants or animals that are extinct.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that some groups of plants and animals are no longer living (extinct) because they were unable to meet their needs for survival.

subject: Science
description: Adaptations and Diversity

subject: Science
description: Create a model habitat which demonstrates interdependence of plants and animals using an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the habitat.

subject: Science
description: Observe and communicate that animals get food from plants or other animals. Plants make their own food and need light to live and grow.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the interdependence of living things and the environment in which they live.

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations using observations to describe and report what animals need to live and grow (food, water, shelter, and space).

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, conduct a structured investigation to determine what plants need to live and grow (water, light, and a place to grow). Measure growth by directly comparing plants with other objects.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of what animals and plants need to live and grow.

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science
description: Use observations to explain that young plants and animals are like but not exactly like their parents (i.e., puppies look similar, but not exactly like their parents).

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, conduct a structured investigation to observe and measure (comparison of lengths) the changes in various individuals of a single plant species from seed germination to adult plant. Record observations using drawing or writing.

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations using observations to describe and model the life cycle (birth, growth, adulthood, death) of a familiar mammal (e.g., dog, squirrel, rabbit, deer).

subject: Science
description: Use informational text or other media to make observations about plants as they change during the life cycle (e.g., germination, growth, reproduction, and death) and use models (e.g., drawing, writing, dramatization, or technology) to communicate findings.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how living things change in form as they go through the general stages of a life cycle.

subject: Science
description: Reproduction and Heredity

subject: Science
description: Identify and describe examples of how animals use their sensory body parts (eyes to detect light and movement, ears to detect sound, skin to detect temperature and touch, tongue to taste, and nose to detect smell).

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to exemplify how animals use their body parts to (a) obtain food and other resources, (b) protect themselves, and (c) move from place to place.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how animals (including humans) use their physical features and their senses to learn about their environment.

subject: Science
description: With teacher support, gain an understanding that scientists are humans who use observations to learn about the natural world. Obtain information from informational text or other media about scientists who have made important observations about living things (e.g. Carl Linnaeus, John James Audubon, Jane Goodall).

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, conduct an investigation of living organisms and nonliving objects in various real-world environments to define characteristics of living organisms that distinguish them from nonliving things (e.g., playground, garden, school grounds).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of living and nonliving things.

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

subject: Science
description: Design a process for cleaning a polluted environment (e.g., simulating an oil spill in the ocean or a flood in a city and creating a solution for containment and/or cleanup). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the environment.

subject: Science
description: Use maps and historical information to identify natural resources in the state connecting (a) how resources are used for human needs and (b) how the use of those resources impacts the environment.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information to exemplify how humans attain, use, and protect renewable and nonrenewable Earth resources.

subject: Science
description: Identify some of Earth's resources that are used in everyday life such as water, wind, soil, forests, oil, natural gas, and minerals and classify as renewable or nonrenewable.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that all materials, energy, and fuels that humans use are derived from natural sources.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science
description: Use graphical representations to communicate the distribution of freshwater and saltwater on Earth (e.g., oceans, lakes, rivers, glaciers, groundwater, or polar ice caps).

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations of how different landforms and surface features result from the location and movement of water on Earth's surface (e.g., watersheds, drainage basins, deltas, or rivers).

subject: Science
description: Develop models to communicate the characteristics of the Earth's major systems, including the geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere (e.g., digital models, illustrations, flip books, diagrams, charts, tables).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how the Earth's systems (i.e., geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere) interact in multiple ways to affect Earth's surface materials and processes.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Systems and Cycles

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast constructive (e.g., deposition, volcano) and destructive (e.g., weathering, erosion, earthquake) processes of the Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models of weathering, erosion, and deposition processes which explain the appearance of various Earth features (e.g., the Grand Canyon, Arches National Park in Utah, Plymouth Bluff in Columbus, or Red Bluff in Marion County, Mississippi).

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to describe the characteristics of Earth's continental landforms and classify landforms as volcanoes, mountains, valleys, canyons, planes, and islands.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate scientific information (e.g. using technology) to describe the four major layers of Earth and the varying compositions of each layer.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the composition of Earth and the processes which change Earth's landforms.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to generate testable hypotheses regarding the formation and location of fossil types, including their presence in some sedimentary rock.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to demonstrate the processes involved in the development of various rock formations, including superposition, and how those formations can fracture and move over time.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct controlled scientific investigations to identify the processes involved in forming the three major types of rock, and investigate common techniques used to identify them.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the various processes involved in the rock cycle, superposition of rock layers, and fossil formation.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Structure and History

subject: Science
description: Define and solve a simple design problem by applying scientific ideas about magnets (e.g., can opener, door latches, paperclip holders, finding studs in walls, magnetized paint). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the magnet.

subject: Science
description: Research and communicate information to explain how magnets are used in everyday life.

subject: Science
description: Plan an experiment to investigate the relationship between a force applied to an object (e.g., friction, gravity) and resulting motion of the object.

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast the effects of different strengths and directions of forces on the motion of an object (e.g., gravity, polarity, attraction, repulsion, or strength).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of magnets and the effects of pushes, pulls, and friction on the motion of objects.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations that particles speed up or slow down with addition or removal of heat.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to communicate the concept that matter is made of particles too small to be seen that move freely around in space (e.g., inflation and shape of a balloon, wind blowing leaves, or dust suspended in the air).

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct scientific investigations to determine how changes in heat (i.e., an increase or decrease) change matter from one state to another (e.g., melting, freezing, condensing, boiling, or evaporating).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the physical properties of matter to explain why matter can change states between a solid, liquid, or gas dependent upon the addition or removal of heat.

subject: Science
description: Organization of Matter and Chemical Interactions

subject: Science
description: Construct scientific argument using evidence from fossils of plants and animals that lived long ago to infer the characteristics of early environments (e.g., marine fossils on dry land, tropical plant fossils in arctic areas, or fossils of extinct organisms in any environment).

subject: Science
description: Define and improve a solution to a problem created by environmental changes and any resulting impacts on the types of density and distribution of plant and animal populations living in the environment (e.g., replanting sea oats in coastal areas or developing or preserving wildlife corridors and green belts). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain how variations in characteristics among organisms of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing (e.g., plants with larger thorns being less likely to be eaten by predators or animals with better camouflage colorations being more likely to survive and bear offspring).

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to predict how natural or man-made changes in a habitat cause plants and animals to respond in different ways, including hibernating, migrating, responding to light, death, or extinction (e.g., sea turtles, the dodo bird, or nocturnal species).

subject: Science
description: Obtain data from informational text to explain how changes in habitats (both those that occur naturally and those caused by organisms) can be beneficial or harmful to the organisms that live there.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how adaptations allow animals to satisfy life needs and respond both physically and behaviorally to their environment.

subject: Science
description: Adaptations and Diversity

subject: Science
description: Research to justify the concept that traits can be influenced by the environment (e.g., stunted growth in normally tall plants due to insufficient water, changes in an arctic fox's fur color due to light and/or temperature, or flamingo plumage).

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from both parent organisms and that variations of these traits exist in groups of similar organisms (e.g., flower colors in pea plants or fur color and pattern in animal offspring).

subject: Science
description: Describe and provide examples of offspring from two parent organisms as containing a combination of inherited traits from both parent organisms.

subject: Science
description: Describe and provide examples of plant and animal offspring from a single parent organism (e.g., bamboo, fern, or starfish) as being an exact replica with identical traits as the parent organism.

subject: Science
description: Identify traits and describe how traits are passed from parent organism(s) to offspring in plants and animals.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that through reproduction, the survival and physical features of plants and animals are inherited traits from parent organisms but can also be influenced by the environment.

subject: Science
description: Reproduction and Heredity

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate examples of physical features or behaviors of vertebrates and invertebrates and how these characteristics help them survive in particular environments (e.g., animals hibernate, migrate, or estivate to stay alive when food is scarce or temperatures are not favorable).

subject: Science
description: Examine evidence to communicate information that the internal and external structures of plant (e.g., thorns, leaves, stems, roots, or colored petals) function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction

subject: Science
description: Examine evidence to communicate information that the internal and external structures of animals (e.g., heart, stomach, bone, lung, brain, skin, ears, appendages) function to support survival, growth, and behavior.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of internal and external structures in plants and animals and how they relate to their growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction within an environment.

subject: Science
description: Investigate how variations of sunlight intensity experienced by each hemisphere (to include the equator and poles) create the four seasons.

subject: Science
description: Construct models and diagrams to illustrate how the tilt of Earth's axis results in differences in intensity of sunlight on the Earth's hemispheres throughout the course of one full revolution around the Sun.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that the seasons are the direct result of the Earth's tilt and the intensity of sunlight on the Earth's hemispheres.

subject: Science
description: Engage in scientific argument based on current evidence to determine whether climate change happens naturally or is being accelerated through the influence of man.

subject: Science
description: Interpret data about the relationship between the release of carbon dioxide from burning fossil fuels into the atmosphere and the presence of greenhouse gases.

subject: Science
description: Read and evaluate scientific or technical information assessing the evidence and bias of each source to explain the causes and effects of climate change.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between natural phenomena, human activity, and global climate change.

subject: Science
description: Interpret topographic maps to predict how local and regional geography affect weather patterns and make them difficult to predict.

subject: Science
description: Research and use models to explain what type of weather (thunderstorms, hurricanes, and tornadoes) results from the movement and interactions of air masses, high and low pressure systems, and frontal boundaries.

subject: Science
description: Analyze models to explain the cause and effect relationship between solar energy and convection and the resulting weather patterns and climate conditions.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation for how climate is determined in an area using global and surface features (e.g. latitude, elevation, shape of the land, distance from water, global winds and ocean currents).

subject: Science
description: Interpret atmospheric data from satellites, radar, and weather maps to predict weather patterns and conditions.

subject: Science
description: Analyze evidence to explain the weather conditions that result from the relationship between the movement of water and air masses.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret weather patterns from various regions to differentiate between weather and climate.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how complex changes in the movement and patterns of air and water molecules caused by the sun, winds, landforms, ocean temperatures, and currents in the atmosphere are major determinants of local and global weather patterns.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Systems and Cycles

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast balanced and unbalanced chemical equations to demonstrate the number of atoms does not change in the reaction.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from investigations to explain why the total mass of the product in an open system appears to be less than the mass of reactants.

subject: Science
description: Conduct simple scientific investigations to show that total mass is not altered during a chemical reaction in a closed system. Compare results of investigations to Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier's discovery of the law of conservation of mass.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the law of conservation of mass.

subject: Science
description: Build a model to explain that chemical reactions can store (formation of bonds) or release energy (breaking of bonds).

subject: Science
description: Collect, organize, and interpret data using various tools (e.g., litmus paper, pH paper, cabbage juice) regarding neutralization of acids and bases using common substances.

subject: Science
description: Design and conduct scientific investigations to support evidence that chemical reactions (e.g., cooking, combustion, rusting, decomposition, photosynthesis, and cellular respiration) have occurred.

subject: Science
description: Analyze evidence from scientific investigations to predict likely outcomes of chemical reactions.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of chemical formulas and common chemical substances to predict the types of reactions and possible outcomes of the reactions.

subject: Science
description: Using the periodic table, make predictions to explain how bonds (ionic and covalent) form between groups of elements (e.g., oxygen gas, ozone, water, table salt, and methane).

subject: Science
description: Describe concepts used to construct chemical formulas (e.g. CH4, H20) to determine the number of atoms in a chemical formula.

subject: Science
description: Predict the properties and interactions of elements using the periodic table (metals, non-metals, reactivity, and conductors).

subject: Science
description: Collect, organize, and interpret data from investigations to identify and analyze the relationships between the physical and chemical properties of elements, atoms, molecules, compounds, solutions, and mixtures.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text to sequence the major discoveries leading to the current atomic model.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models that explain the structure of an atom.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the proper use of the periodic table to predict and identify elemental properties and how elements interact.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to explain how density of matter (observable in various objects) is affected by a change in heat and/or pressure.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence from multiple scientific investigations to communicate the relationships between pressure, volume, density, and temperature of a gas.

subject: Science
description: Make predictions about the effect of temperature and pressure on the relative motion of atoms and molecules (speed, expansion, and condensation) relative to recent breakthroughs in polymer and materials science (e.g. self-healing protective films, silicone computer processors, pervious/porous concrete).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding about the effects of temperature and pressure on physical state, molecular motion, and molecular interactions.

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast chemical and physical properties (e.g., combustion, oxidation, pH, solubility, reaction with water).

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret qualitative data to describe substances using chemical properties (the ability to burn or rust).

subject: Science
description: Collect and evaluate qualitative data to describe substances using physical properties (state, boiling/melting point, density, heat/electrical conductivity, color, and magnetic properties).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the physical and chemical properties of matter.

subject: Science
description: Organization of Matter and Chemical Interactions

subject: Science
description: Design solutions for sustaining the health of ecosystems to maintain biodiversity and the resources needed by humans for survival (e.g., water purification, nutrient recycling, prevention of soil erosion, and prevention or management of invasive species).

subject: Science
description: Explain how disruptions in cycles (e.g., water, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen) affect biodiversity and ecosystem services (e.g., water, food, and medications) which are needed to sustain human life on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Use models to describe how food molecules (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins) are processed through chemical reactions using oxygen (aerobic) to form new molecules.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain how the processes of photosynthesis, and cellular respiration (aerobic and anaerobic) work together to meet the needs of plants and animals.

subject: Science
description: Analyze diagrams to provide evidence of the importance of the cycling of water, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen through ecosystems to organisms.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the importance that matter cycles between living and nonliving parts of the ecosystem to sustain life on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from the surface features of the Sun (e.g., photosphere, corona, sunspots, prominences, and solar flares) to predict how these features may affect Earth.

subject: Science
description: Design models representing motions within the Sun-Earth-Moon system to explain phenomena observed from the Earth's surface (positions of celestial bodies, day and year, moon phases, solar and lunar eclipses, and tides).

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations for how gravity affects the motion of objects in the solar system and tides on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate information to model and compare the characteristics and movements of objects in the solar system (including planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and meteors).

subject: Science
description: Evaluate modern techniques used to explore our solar system's position in the universe.

subject: Science
description: Use graphical displays or models to explain the hierarchical structure (stars, galaxies, galactic clusters) of the universe.

subject: Science
description: Obtain, evaluate, and summarize past and present theories and evidence to explain the formation and composition of the universe.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of Earth's place in the universe and the interactions of the solar system (sun, planets, their moons, comets, and asteroids) using evidence from multiple scientific resources to explain how these objects are held in orbit around the Sun because of its gravitational pull.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Universe

subject: Science
description: Determine the relationships between the concepts of potential, kinetic, and thermal energy.

subject: Science
description: Investigate forces (gravity, friction, drag, lift, thrust) acting on objects (e.g., airplane, bicycle helmets). Use data to explain the differences between the forces in various environments.

subject: Science
description: Conduct investigations to predict and explain the motion of an object according to its position, direction, speed, and acceleration.

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast magnetic, electric, frictional, and gravitational forces.

subject: Science
description: Investigate and communicate ways to manipulate applied/frictional forces to improve movement of objects on various surfaces (e.g., athletic shoes, wheels on cars).

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical computation and diagrams to calculate the sum of forces acting on various objects.

subject: Science
description: Use an engineering design process to create or improve safety devices (e.g., seat belts, car seats, helmets) by applying Newton's Laws of motion. Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the safety device.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of Newton's laws of motion using real world models and examples.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Engage in scientific arguments to support claims that bacteria (Archaebacteria and Eubacteria) and viruses can be both helpful and harmful to other organisms and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Conduct investigations using a microscope or multimedia source to compare the characteristics of protists (euglena, paramecium, amoeba) and the methods they use to obtain energy and move through their environment (e.g., pond water).

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from observations to describe how fungi obtain energy and respond to stimuli (e.g., bread mold, rotting plant material).

subject: Science
description: Use classification methods to explore the diversity of organisms in kingdoms (animals, plants, fungi, protists, bacteria). Support claims that organisms have shared structural and behavioral characteristics.

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast modern classification techniques (e.g., analyzing genetic material) to the historical practices used by scientists such as Aristotle and Carolus Linnaeus.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of classification tools and models such as dichotomous keys to classify representative organisms based on the characteristics of the kingdoms: Archaebacteria, Eubacteria, Protists, Fungi, Plants, and Animals.

subject: Science
description: Adaptation and Diversity

subject: Science
description: Develop and use food chains, webs, and pyramids to analyze how energy is transferred through an ecosystem from producers (autotrophs) to consumers (heterotrophs, including humans) to decomposers.

subject: Science
description: Investigate organism interactions in a competitive or mutually beneficial relationship (predation, competition, cooperation, or symbiotic relationships).

subject: Science
description: Analyze cause and effect relationships to explore how changes in the physical environment (limiting factors, natural disasters) can lead to population changes within an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to describe the levels of organization within ecosystems (species, populations, communities, ecosystems, and biomes).

subject: Science
description: Use scientific reasoning to explain differences between biotic and abiotic factors that demonstrate what living organisms need to survive.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the relationships among survival, environmental changes, and diversity as they relate to the interactions of organisms, populations, and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to show relationships among the increasing complexity of multicellular organisms (cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, organisms) and how they serve the needs of the organism.

subject: Science
description: Provide evidence that organisms are unicellular or multicellular.

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast different cells in order to classify them as a protist, fungus, plant, or animal.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to explain how specific cellular components (cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, vacuole, and mitochondria) function together to support the life of prokaryotic and eukaryotic organisms to include plants, animals, fungi, protists, and bacteria (not to include biochemical function of cells or cell part).

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate evidence to support the cell theory.

subject: Science
description: Use argument supported by evidence in order to distinguish between living and non-living things, including viruses and bacteria.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that living things range from simple to complex organisms, are organized hierarchically, and function as whole living systems.

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

subject: Science
description: Use technology or informational text to investigate, evaluate, and communicate various forms of clean energy generation.

subject: Science
description: Organize simple data sets to compare energy and pollution output of various traditional, non-renewable resources (e.g. coal, crude oil, wood).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the various sources of energy used for human needs along with their effectiveness and possible impacts.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information about severe weather phenomena (e.g., thunderstorms, hurricanes, or tornadoes) to explain steps humans can take to reduce the impact of severe weather events.

subject: Science
description: Research and explain how systems (i.e., the atmosphere, geosphere, and/or hydrosphere), interact and support life in the biosphere.

subject: Science
description: Construct scientific arguments from evidence to support claims that human activities, such as conservation efforts or pollution, affect the land, oceans, and atmosphere of Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models of natural processes to explain the effect of the movement of water on the ocean shore zone, including beaches, barrier islands, estuaries, and inlets (e.g., marshes, bays, lagoons, fjord, or sound).

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to describe and predict how natural processes (e.g., weathering, erosion, deposition, earthquakes, tsunamis, hurricanes, or storms) affect Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how natural processes and human activities affect the features of Earth's landforms and oceans.

subject: Science
description: Design weather instruments utilized to measure weather conditions (e.g., barometer, hygrometer, rain gauge, anemometer, or wind vane). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the weather instrument.

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations about regional climate differences using maps and long-term data from various regions.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data (e.g., temperature, precipitation, wind speed/direction, relative humidity, or cloud types) to predict changes in weather over time.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of weather and climate patterns.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to explain how the sun's energy drives the water cycle. (e.g., evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff, and groundwater).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how the water cycle is propelled by the sun's energy.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Systems and Cycles

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information about scientists who pioneered in the science of sound, (e.g., Alexander Graham Bell, Robert Boyle, Daniel Bernoulli, and Guglielmo Marconi).

subject: Science
description: In relation to how sound is perceived by humans, analyze and interpret data from observations and measurements to report how changes in vibration affect the pitch and volume of sound.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct scientific investigations to test how different variables affect the properties of sound (i.e., pitch and volume).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the properties of sound as a form of energy.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct scientific investigations to explain how light behaves when it strikes transparent, translucent, and opaque materials.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to communicate how light travels and behaves when it strikes an object, including reflection, refraction, and absorption.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information to explain how the visibility of an object is related to light.

subject: Science
description: Construct scientific evidence to support the claim that white light is made up of different colors. Include the work of Sir Isaac Newton to communicate results.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the properties of light as forms of energy.

subject: Science
description: Design a device that converts any form of energy from one form to another form (e.g., construct a musical instrument that will convert vibrations to sound by controlling varying pitches, a solar oven that will convert energy from the sun to heat energy, or a simple circuit that can be used to complete a task). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the device.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text and technology resources to communicate technological breakthroughs made by historical figures in electricity (e.g. Alessandro Volta, Michael Faraday, Nicola Tesla, Thomas Edison, incandescent light bulbs, batteries, Light Emitting Diodes).

subject: Science
description: Develop models that demonstrate the path of an electric current in a complete, simple circuit (e.g., lighting a light bulb or making a sound).

subject: Science
description: Develop models demonstrating how heat and electrical energy can be transformed into other forms of energy (e.g., motion, sound, heat, or light).

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct scientific investigations to classify different materials as either an insulator or conductor of electricity.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information to compare how different processes (including burning, friction, and electricity) serve as sources of heat energy.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the common sources and uses of heat and electric energy and the materials used to transfer heat and electricity.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to explain the unique and diverse life cycles of organisms other than humans (e.g., flowering plants, frogs, or butterflies) including commonalities (e.g., birth, growth, reproduction, or death).

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast life cycles of familiar plants and animals.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of life cycles, including familiar plants and animals (e.g., reptiles, amphibians, or birds).

subject: Science
description: Reproduction and Heredity

subject: Science
description: Using informational text, investigate how scientific fields, medical specialties, and research methods help us find new ways to maintain a healthy body and lifestyle (e.g. diet, exercise, vaccines, and mental health).

subject: Science
description: Research and communicate how noninfectious diseases (e.g. diabetes, heart disease) and infectious diseases (e.g. cold, flu) serve to disrupt the function of the body system.

subject: Science
description: Construct models of organ systems (e.g. circulatory, digestive, respiratory, muscular, skeletal, nervous) to demonstrate both the unique function of the system and how multiple organs and organ systems work together to accomplish more complex functions.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate data to describe patterns that indicate the nature of relationships between human organ systems, which interact with one another to control digestion, respiration, circulation, excretion, movement, coordination, and protection from infection.

subject: Science
description: Use technology or other resources to research and discover general system function (e.g., machines, water cycle) as they relate to human organ systems and identify organs that work together to create organ systems.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the organization, functions, and interconnections of the major human body systems.

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, investigate possible solutions to prevent or repair soil erosion.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text, other media, and first-hand observations to investigate and communicate the process and consequences of soil erosion.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text and other media to summarize and communicate how Earth materials are used (e.g., soil and water to grow plants; rocks to make roads, walls or building; or sand to make glass).

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to identify and classify everyday objects that are resources from the Earth (e.g., drinking water, granite countertops, clay dishes, wood furniture, or gas grill). Classify these objects as renewable and nonrenewable resources.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text, other media, and first-hand observations to investigate, analyze and compare the properties of Earth materials (including rocks, soils, sand, and water).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how humans use Earth's resources.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science
description: Create a model that will demonstrate the observable pattern of motion of the Sun or Moon. Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the model.

subject: Science
description: Use informational text and other media to observe, describe and predict the visual patterns of motion of the Sun (sunrise, sunset) and Moon (phases).

subject: Science
description: With teacher support, gain an understanding that scientists are humans who use observations and experiments to learn about space. Obtain information from informational text or other media about scientists who have made important discoveries about objects in space (e.g., Galileo Galilei, Johannes Kepler, George Ellery Hale, Jill Tarter) or the development of technologies (e.g., various telescopes and detection devices, computer modeling, and space exploration).

subject: Science
description: Observe and compare the details in images of the moon and planets using the perspective of the naked eye, telescopes, and data from space exploration.

subject: Science
description: With teacher guidance, observe, describe, and predict the seasonal patterns of sunrise and sunset. Collect, represent, and interpret data from internet sources to communicate findings.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that there are many stars that can be observed in the night sky and the Sun is the Earth's closest star.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the appearance, movements, and patterns of the sun, moon, and stars.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Universe

subject: Science
description: Develop a plan to change the force (push or pull) of friction to solve a human problem (e.g., improve the ride on a playground slide or make a toy car or truck go faster). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the plan.

subject: Science
description: Generate and answer questions about the relationship between (1) friction and the motion of objects and (2) friction and the production of heat.

subject: Science
description: Conduct a structured investigation to collect, represent, and analyze data from observations and measurements to demonstrate the effects of pushes and pulls with different strengths and directions. Communicate findings (e.g., models or technology).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how the motion of objects is affected by pushes, pulls, and friction on an object.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Construct scientific arguments to support claims that some changes to matter caused by heating can be reversed, and some changes cannot be reversed.

subject: Science
description: Compare the weight of solid objects and the volume of liquid objects. Analyze and communicate findings.

subject: Science
description: Compare and measure the length of solid objects using technology and mathematical representations. Analyze and communicate findings.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the properties of matter.

subject: Science
description: Organization of Matter and Chemical Interactions

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate examples of physical features or behaviors of vertebrates and invertebrates and how these characteristics help them survive in particular environments, (e.g., animals hibernate, migrate, or estivate to stay alive when food is scarce or temperatures are not favorable).

subject: Science
description: Examine evidence to communicate information that the internal and external structures of plant (e.g., thorns, leaves, stems, roots, or colored petals) function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.

subject: Science
description: Examine evidence to communicate information that the internal and external structures of animals (e.g., heart, stomach, bone, lung, brain, skin, ears, appendages) function to support survival, growth, and behavior.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of internal and external structures in plants and animals and how they relate to their growth, survival, behavior, and reproduction within an environment.

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

subject: Science
description: Create a solution exemplified by animal adaptations to solve a human problem in a specific environment (e.g., snowshoes are like hare's feet or flippers are like duck's feet). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the solution.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate and communicate findings from informational text or other media to describe how plants and animals use adaptations to survive (e.g., ducks use webbed feet to swim in lakes and ponds, cacti have waxy coatings and spines to grow in the desert) in distinct environments (e.g., polar lands, saltwater and freshwater, desert, rainforest, woodlands).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the ways animals adapt to their environment in order to survive.

subject: Science
description: Adaptations and Diversity

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to find evidence where plants and animals compete or cooperate with other plants and animals for food or space. Present findings (i.e., using technology or models).

subject: Science
description: Evaluate and communicate findings from informational text or other media to describe and to compare how animals interact with other animals and plants in the environment (i.e., predator-prey relationships, herbivore, carnivore, omnivore).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the interdependence of living things.

subject: Science
description: Construct scientific arguments to explain how animals can make major changes (e.g., beaver dams obstruct streams, or large deer populations destroying crops) and minor changes to their environments (e.g., ant hills, crawfish burrows, mole tunnels). Communicate findings.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate and communicate findings from informational text or other media to describe how animals change and respond to rapid or slow changes in their environment (fire, pollution, changes in tide, availability of food/water).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the interdependence of living things and the environment in which they live.

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how living things change in form as they go through the general stages of a life cycle.

subject: Science
description: Reproduction and Heredity

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast physical characteristics that distinguish classes of vertebrates (i.e., reptiles compared to amphibians).

subject: Science
description: Classify vertebrates (mammals, fish, birds, amphibians, and reptiles) based on their physical characteristics.

subject: Science
description: Compare and sort groups of animals with backbones (vertebrates) from groups of animals without backbones (invertebrates).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the classification of animals based on physical characteristics.

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

subject: Science
description: Create a device that will collect free water to meet a human need (e.g., household drinking water, watering plants/animals, cleaning). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the device.

subject: Science
description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the use and quality of water in the local environment.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate informational texts and other media to generate and answer questions about water sources and human uses of clean water.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of human dependence on clean and renewable water resources.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

description: With teacher guidance, plan and conduct a structured investigation to determine how the movement of water can change the shape of the land on earth.

subject: Science
description: Generate and answer questions to explain the patterns and location of frozen and liquid bodies of water on earth using maps, globes, or other media.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of models (drawings or maps) to describe how water and land are distributed on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information about severe weather conditions to explain why certain safety precautions are necessary.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to predict weather conditions associated with seasonal patterns and changes.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from observations and measurements to describe local weather conditions (including temperature, wind, and forms of precipitation).

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Earth's Systems and Cycles

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that vibrations create sound (e.g., pluck a guitar string) and that sound can create vibrations (e.g., feeling sound through a speaker).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of sound.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Construct explanations using first-hand observations or other media to describe how reflected light makes an object visible.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that light is required to make objects visible.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Create a solution for an agricultural problem (i.e. pollination, seed dispersal, over-crowding). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the solution.

subject: Science
description: Describe how the different characteristics of plants help them to survive in distinct environments (e.g., rain forest, desert, grasslands, forests).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the ways plants adapt to their environment in order to survive.

subject: Science
description: Adaptations and Diversity

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science

subject: Science

Use drawings, writing, or models to communicate findings.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how living things change in form as they go through the general stages of a life cycle.

subject: Science
description: Reproduction and Heredity

subject: Science

Obtain information from informational text or other media about scientists who have made important observations about plants (e.g., Theophrastus, Gregor Mendel, George Washington Carver, Katherine Esau).

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Hierarchical Organization

Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the disaster plan.

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Describe how constellations appear to move from Earth's perspective throughout the seasons (e.g., Ursa Major, Ursa Minor, and Orion).

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to argue why the sun appears brighter than other stars.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use scaled models of Earth's solar system to demonstrate the size, composition (i.e., rock or gas), location, and order of the planets as they orbit the Sun.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the locations of objects in the universe.

subject: Science
description: Earth and the Universe

description: Design a system to increase the effects of friction on the motion of an object (e.g., non-slip surfaces or vehicle braking systems or flaps on aircraft wings). Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the system.

subject: Science
description: Predict how a change of force, mass, and/or friction affects the motion of an object to convert potential energy into kinetic energy.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct scientific investigations to test the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the speed and/or direction of objects in motion.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to explain how the amount or type of force, both contact and non-contact, affects the motion of an object.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information describing gravity's effect on an object.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the factors that affect the motion of an object through a study of Newton's Laws of Motion.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to support claims that when two substances are mixed, the total weight of matter is conserved.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Analyze and communicate the results of chemical changes that result in the formation of new materials (e.g., decaying, burning, rusting, or cooking).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the difference between physical and chemical changes.

subject: Science
description: Design an effective system (e.g., sifting, filtration, evaporation, magnetic attraction, or floatation) for separating various mixtures. Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the system.

subject: Science
description: Investigate how different variables (e.g., temperature change, stirring, particle size, or surface area) affect the rate at which a solute will dissolve.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to communicate that the concentration of a solution is determined by the relative amount of solute versus solvent in various mixtures.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate scientific information to describe what happens to the properties of substances in mixtures and solutions.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of mixtures and solutions.

subject: Science
description: Design a vessel that can safely transport a dense substance (e.g., syrup, coins, marbles) through water at various distances and under variable conditions. Use an engineering design process to define the problem, design, construct, evaluate, and improve the vessel.

subject: Science
description: Make and test predictions about how the density of an object affects whether the object sinks or floats when placed in a liquid.

subject: Science
description: Analyze matter through observations and measurements to classify materials (e.g., powders, metals, minerals, or liquids) based on their properties (e.g., color, hardness, reflectivity, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, response to magnetic forces, solubility, or density).

subject: Science
description: Collect, analyze, and interpret data from measurements of the physical properties of solids, liquids, and gases (e.g., volume, shape, movement, and spacing of particles).

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate scientific information to describe basic physical properties of atoms and molecules.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the physical properties of matter.

subject: Science
description: Organization of Matter and Chemical Interactions

subject: Science
description: Communicate scientific or technical information that explains human positions in food webs and our potential impacts on these systems.

subject: Science
description: Design and interpret models of food webs to justify what effects the removal or the addition of a species (i.e., introduced or invasive) would have on a specific population and/or the ecosystem as a whole.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a food chain model to classify organisms as producers, consumers, or decomposers. Trace the energy flow to explain how each group of organisms obtains energy.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of a healthy ecosystem with a stable web of life and the roles of living things within a food chain and/or food web, including producers, primary and secondary consumers, and decomposers.

subject: Science
description: Analyze environments that do not receive direct sunlight and devise explanations as to how photosynthesis occurs, either naturally or artificially.

subject: Science
description: Research and communicate the basic process of photosynthesis that is used by plants to convert light energy into chemical energy that can be stored and released to fuel an organism's activities.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of photosynthesis and the transfer of energy from the sun into chemical energy necessary for plant growth and survival.

subject: Science
description: Ecology and Interdependence

subject: Science
description: Using an engineering design process, develop a system to capture and distribute thermal energy that makes renewable energy more readily available and reduces human impact on the environment (e.g., building solar water heaters, conserving home energy).

subject: Science
description: Using scientific data, debate the societal advantages and disadvantages of technological advancements in renewable energy sources.

subject: Science
description: Create and defend a proposal for reducing the environmental effects humans have on Earth (e.g., population increases, consumer demands, chemical pollution, deforestation, and change in average annual temperature).

subject: Science
description: Read and evaluate scientific information about advancements in renewable and nonrenewable resources. Propose and defend ways to decrease national and global dependency on nonrenewable resources.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that a decrease in natural resources is directly related to the increase in human population on Earth and must be conserved.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Resources

subject: Science
description: Using an engineering design process, create mechanisms to improve community resilience, which safeguard against natural hazards (e.g., building restrictions in flood or tidal zones, regional watershed management, Firewise construction).

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast technologies that predict natural hazards to identify which types of technologies are most effective.

subject: Science
description: Research and map various types of natural hazards to determine their impact on society.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of natural hazards (volcanic eruptions, severe weather, earthquakes) and construct explanations for why some hazards are predictable and others are not.

subject: Science
description: Explain the interconnected relationship between surface water and groundwater.

subject: Science
description: Design and conduct investigations to evaluate the chemical and physical processes involved in the formation of soils.

subject: Science
description: Use models that demonstrate convergent and divergent plate movements that are responsible for most landforms and the distribution of most rocks and minerals within Earth's crust.

subject: Science
description: Research and assess the credibility of scientific ideas to debate and discuss how Earth's constructive and destructive processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.

subject: Science
description: Map land and water patterns from various time periods and use rocks and fossils to report evidence of how Earth's plates have moved great distances, collided, and spread apart.

subject: Science
description: Explore and debate theories of plate tectonics to form conclusions about past and current movements of rocks at Earth's surface throughout history.

subject: Science
description: Investigate and explain how the flow of Earth's internal energy drives the cycling of matter through convection currents between Earth's surface and the deep interior causing plate movements.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding that physical processes and major geological events (e.g., plate movement, volcanic activity, mountain building, weathering, erosion) are powered by the Sun and the Earth's internal heat and have occurred over millions of years.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Systems and Cycles

subject: Science
description: Use research and evidence to document how evolution has been shaped both gradually and through mass extinction by Earth's varying geological conditions (e.g., climate change, meteor impacts, and volcanic eruptions).

subject: Science
description: Construct and analyze scientific arguments to support claims that most fossil evidence is an indication of the diversity of life that was present on Earth and that relationships exist between past and current life forms.

subject: Science
description: Create a model of the processes involved in the rock cycle and relate it to the fossil record.

subject: Science
description: Use scientific evidence to create a timeline of Earth's history that depicts relative dates from index fossil records and layers of rock (strata).

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of geological evidence to analyze patterns in Earth's major events, processes, and evolution in history.

subject: Science
description: Earth's Structure and History

subject: Science
description: Compare and contrast the behavior of sound and light waves to determine which types of waves need a medium for transmission.

subject: Science
description: Research the historical significance of wave technology to explain how digitized tools have evolved to encode and transmit information (e.g., telegraph, cell phones, and wireless computer networks).

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate scientific information to explain the relationship between seeing color and the transmission, absorption, or reflection of light waves by various materials.

subject: Science

subject: Science
description: Use scientific processes to plan and conduct controlled investigations to conclude sound is a wave phenomenon that is characterized by amplitude and frequency.

subject: Science
description: Conduct simple investigations about the performance of waves to describe their behavior (e.g., refraction, reflection, transmission, and absorption) as they interact with various materials (e.g., lenses, mirrors, and prisms).

subject: Science
description: Investigate research-based mechanisms for capturing and converting wave energy (frequency, amplitude, wavelength, and speed) into electrical energy.

subject: Science
description: Collect, organize, and interpret data about the characteristics of sound and light waves to construct explanations about the relationship between matter and energy.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the properties, behaviors, and application of waves.

subject: Science
description: Motions, Forces, and Energy

subject: Science
description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare and contrast embryological and homologous/analogous structures across multiple species to identify evolutionary relationships.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and evaluate scientific information to explain that separated populations, that remain separated, can evolve through mutations to become a new species (speciation).

subject: Science
description: Construct written and verbal explanations to describe how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some organisms' probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data (e.g. pictures, graphs) to explain how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.

subject: Science
description: Students will demonstrate an understanding of how similarities and differences among living and extinct species provide evidence that changes have occurred in organisms over time and that similarity of characteristics provides evidence of common ancestry.

subject: Science

description: Integrate and evaluate content presented in diverse media and formats, including visually and quantitatively, as well as in words.

subject: Language Arts
description: Assess how point of view or purpose shapes the content and style of a text.
